past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 12 years ago, suboptimally controlled
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 6 years ago
Obstructive sleep apnea, uses CPAP inconsistently
Left ventricular hypertrophy noted on prior echocardiogram 2 years ago
Remote history of anterior STEMI treated with PCI to LAD 5 years ago

medications.indication: Dual antiplatelet therapy post-PCI (continued due to stent history)

family_history: Father had a pacemaker placed at age 68 for unknown cardiac condition, died at age 74 of heart failure
Mother alive at 84 with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Paternal uncle had sudden cardiac death at age 58

social_history.tobacco: Active smoker, 25-pack-year history, currently smoking half a pack per day
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a second-floor apartment
social_history.exercise: Sedentary lifestyle, reports becoming easily winded climbing stairs over the past 4-5 months

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, able to perform serial sevens (100, 93, 86, 79, 72). Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No confusion at time of evaluation, approximately 45 minutes after the most recent episode.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam reveals mild arteriolar narrowing and AV nicking bilaterally consistent with chronic hypertension; no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in all three divisions bilaterally. Jaw strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue protrudes midline, no fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal, no rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups tested bilaterally including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, hip flexors, knee extensors and flexors, ankle dorsiflexors, and plantarflexors. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact in all extremities. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense mildly diminished at bilateral malleoli.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing normal. Rapid alternating movements intact. No intention tremor.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or evidence of lateral tongue biting. No urinary incontinence reported during any episode. Mild abrasion on the left elbow from a fall during the second episode. No other external injuries.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden loss of consciousness with brief shaking movements
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Darnell Crawford is a 62-year-old man with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, obstructive sleep apnea, and prior anterior STEMI who presents to the emergency department after his third episode of sudden loss of consciousness in approximately six weeks. He was brought in by ambulance after collapsing at a grocery store approximately 90 minutes ago. A bystander witnessed the event and reported that the patient was standing in the checkout line when he suddenly dropped to the ground without any preceding stumble or warning cry. The witness describes that after falling, the patient exhibited brief jerking movements of his arms and legs lasting approximately 10-15 seconds, then lay still and appeared pale. He regained consciousness within approximately 30-40 seconds of the onset, was immediately conversational, and asked where he was. He denied any preceding lightheadedness, chest pain, palpitations, or aura. He did not bite his tongue and there was no urinary incontinence. By the time EMS arrived approximately 8 minutes later, the patient was sitting upright and reported feeling entirely normal. His two prior episodes occurred approximately six weeks ago and three weeks ago. The first event happened while he was watching television at home; he lost consciousness without warning and his neighbor, who was visiting, noted approximately 5 seconds of bilateral arm jerking before he spontaneously recovered. He did not seek medical attention for that episode. The second event occurred while climbing stairs in his apartment building; he suddenly blacked out, fell backwards down three stairs, and sustained a left elbow abrasion. Again, no prodrome, no post-ictal confusion beyond a brief period of disorientation lasting less than one minute, and no tongue biting or incontinence. He saw his primary care physician after the second event, who referred him for neurology evaluation with concern for new-onset seizures. He was seen briefly in a neurology clinic one week ago but no EEG had yet been performed. He reports increasing fatigue and exercise intolerance over the past several months, noting that he becomes short of breath climbing one flight of stairs. He has not noticed palpitations but admits he has not been monitoring his pulse. He denies any recent medication changes, missed doses, fever, or acute illness.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric bilaterally, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythms: Low to medium amplitude beta activity, symmetric
background.sleep_features: Stage II sleep captured with vertex sharp transients and sleep spindles, no abnormalities during sleep

artifacts.description: Intermittent, minimal, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity
Eye blink and slow lateral eye movement artifact during drowsiness

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric bilateral slowing during hyperventilation, returning to baseline within 30 seconds. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response at 12-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and asleep EEG. No epileptiform abnormalities identified. No electrographic seizures captured during this 45-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording. Interictal sensitivity approximately 50% for a single routine EEG. No events captured during the recording.

ecg.rhythm: Sinus bradycardia with bifascicular block (right bundle branch block and left anterior fascicular block)
findings: Sinus bradycardia at 52 bpm
First-degree AV block (PR interval 224 ms)
Right bundle branch block (rsR' pattern in V1-V2, wide slurred S wave in I and V6)
Left anterior fascicular block (left axis deviation with rS pattern in leads II, III, aVF)
Borderline QTc prolongation at 486 ms
Non-specific ST-T wave changes in the anterior leads, likely secondary to RBBB
No acute ST elevation or depression to suggest acute ischemia
ecg.interpretation: Sinus bradycardia with trifascicular disease pattern: first-degree AV block combined with bifascicular block (RBBB and left anterior fascicular block). This conduction pattern indicates extensive disease of the His-Purkinje system and carries a significant risk of progression to complete heart block. The borderline QTc prolongation may be partially attributable to the wide QRS.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL (L), Potassium 3.2 mEq/L (L), Chloride 97 mEq/L (L), Glucose 173 mg/dL (H), BNP 208 pg/mL (H), HbA1c 7.8 % (H).

Echocardiogram.value: Hypokinesis of the mid-to-apical anterior wall and apex
Mild sclerosis, no stenosis, no regurgitation

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Left ventricular ejection fraction 50% % (H), Interventricular septum thickness 1.3 cm (H), Posterior wall thickness 1.2 cm (H), Left atrial diameter 4.1 cm (H), Regional wall motion Hypokinesis of the mid-to-apical anterior wall and apex qualitative (H), Aortic valve Mild sclerosis, no stenosis, no regurgitation qualitative (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Regional wall motion: Hypokinesis of the mid-to-apical anterior wall and apex qualitative (L)
Aortic valve: Mild sclerosis, no stenosis, no regurgitation qualitative (H)

output.rhythm: Predominantly sinus rhythm with bifascicular block; three episodes of paroxysmal high-grade second-degree AV block (Mobitz type II) with pauses up to 5.8 seconds
findings: Baseline sinus bradycardia averaging 54 bpm with bifascicular block pattern (RBBB + LAFB)
Three episodes of paroxysmal Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with 2:1 and 3:1 conduction ratios
Longest RR pause of 5.8 seconds at 14:32 (corresponding to patient-reported near-syncope while walking)
Second significant pause of 4.1 seconds at 22:15 during sleep
Third episode at 09:47 with 3.6-second pause, patient asymptomatic
Average heart rate 54 bpm, minimum 28 bpm (during longest pause), maximum 88 bpm (brief exertion)
Frequent isolated premature ventricular complexes (184 in 48 hours), no couplets or ventricular tachycardia
No atrial fibrillation or flutter captured
output.interpretation: The paroxysmal nature of the high-grade block explains why the resting ECG shows only bifascicular block with first-degree AV block, and the arrhythmia was not initially captured. This represents a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation.

Tilt_table_test.value: Not elicited after 45 minutes of tilt including isoproterenol provocation

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Baseline supine BP 146/86 mmHg (H), Baseline supine HR 54 bpm (H).

output.rhythm: Sinus rhythm with bifascicular block at baseline; exercise-induced progression to 2:1 AV block at peak exertion

intervals.PR: 226 ms at rest, AV dissociation at peak exercise

findings: Baseline sinus rhythm at 54 bpm with RBBB, LAFB, and first-degree AV block
During Stage 2 of Bruce protocol, atrial rate increased appropriately to 118 bpm but AV conduction deteriorated to 2:1 block with ventricular rate of 59 bpm
Patient developed lightheadedness and presyncope at 2:1 AV block, test terminated at 5 minutes 30 seconds
Blood pressure dropped from 152/88 to 110/68 at peak exercise
No exercise-induced ventricular arrhythmias
No ischemic ST changes beyond those attributable to baseline RBBB
Recovery: gradual return to 1:1 conduction over 4 minutes post-exercise
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test demonstrates exercise-induced deterioration of AV conduction from first-degree AV block to 2:1 Mobitz type II block.

signal_characteristics.T1: Elevated native T1 in scar territory
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Transmural enhancement in mid-to-apical anterior wall consistent with prior LAD territory infarction

findings.location: Basal interventricular septum extending toward membranous septum

signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Patchy mid-wall enhancement in basal septum at level of bundle of His

signal_characteristics.T1: Mildly elevated diffuse native T1, consistent with diffuse interstitial fibrosis

additional_observations: No evidence of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy
No evidence of cardiac sarcoidosis pattern (no multifocal patchy enhancement)
No evidence of amyloidosis (no diffuse subendocardial enhancement)
Aortic valve calcification noted on cine imaging
output.impression: Cardiac MRI reveals two distinct pathologic processes contributing to conduction system disease: (1) chronic transmural anterior MI scar in the LAD territory, and (2) patchy mid-wall fibrosis in the basal interventricular septum at the level of the His bundle and proximal bundle branches. The septal fibrosis likely represents a combination of hypertensive interstitial fibrosis and ischemic injury to the conduction system, providing a structural substrate for the patient's bifascicular block and intermittent high-grade AV block. LVEF mildly reduced at 48%. No features of infiltrative cardiomyopathy or sarcoidosis. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Scattered periventricular and deep white matter

additional_observations: No mass lesion or enhancing lesion
output.impression: No acute intracranial pathology. No structural lesion to explain the patient's transient loss of consciousness episodes. The brain MRI does not support a neurological etiology for the recurrent syncope. Clinical correlation recommended.

Electrophysiology_study.value: Block occurs below His at pacing rate 120 bpm
Non-sustained polymorphic VT (3 beats) with triple extrastimuli

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: HV interval 82 ms (H), Infra-Hisian block with atrial pacing Block occurs below His at pacing rate 120 bpm qualitative (H), Inducible ventricular tachycardia Non-sustained polymorphic VT (3 beats) with triple extrastimuli qualitative (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Infra-Hisian block with atrial pacing: Block occurs below His at pacing rate 120 bpm qualitative (L)
Inducible ventricular tachycardia: Non-sustained polymorphic VT (3 beats) with triple extrastimuli qualitative (H)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to intermittent high-grade AV block (Mobitz type II) in the setting of trifascicular disease

differential.key_distinguishing: Brief convulsive movements (convulsive syncope) mimic seizure but last <15 seconds, no post-ictal confusion >1 minute, no tongue biting, no urinary incontinence, normal EEG. Convulsive syncope is caused by cerebral hypoperfusion, not epileptiform activity.
No identifiable triggers, no prodrome of nausea/warmth/diaphoresis, episodes occur in varied positions (seated, standing, climbing stairs), age and cardiac risk factors favor cardiac etiology.
Events not related to positional changes (one occurred while seated). Although patient takes antihypertensives, the clinical pattern does not fit. Tilt table test is negative.
Prior MI is a risk factor for VT, but episodes are very brief (seconds), Holter shows no VT, and the documented arrhythmia is AV block. VT remains in the differential until excluded by monitoring.
Echocardiogram shows only mild sclerosis without stenosis. No systolic murmur. Syncope from aortic stenosis is typically exertional with preserved consciousness until advanced disease.

optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sinus bradycardia with bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) and first-degree AV block, indicating trifascicular disease

optimal_actions.action: Place on continuous cardiac telemetry monitoring and establish IV access
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Continuous monitoring to capture paroxysmal conduction disturbances

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, magnesium, troponin, BNP, TSH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia from thiazide diuretic; elevated BNP; no acute MI

optimal_actions.action: Correct electrolyte abnormalities: IV magnesium sulfate and oral potassium supplementation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Correct hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia to reduce risk of arrhythmia

optimal_actions.action: Hold hydrochlorothiazide (contributing to electrolyte depletion) and consider discontinuing if alternative antihypertensive can be used
Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform abnormalities given the reported convulsive movements
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG, no epileptiform discharges, supporting cardiac rather than epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Request cardiology consultation for evaluation of conduction disease and pacemaker candidacy
Arrange prolonged cardiac monitoring (48-72 hour Holter or inpatient telemetry) to capture intermittent high-grade AV block
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Paroxysmal Mobitz type II AV block with hemodynamically significant pauses

optimal_actions.action: Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to assess for structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mildly reduced LVEF, LVH, prior anterior wall motion abnormality, aortic sclerosis

optimal_actions.action: Admit to telemetry unit for monitoring and plan permanent pacemaker implantation

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to identify trifascicular disease pattern
Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring given high-risk ECG and recurrent syncope
Recognize the bifascicular block with first-degree AV block as trifascicular disease with risk of complete heart block
Obtain prolonged cardiac monitoring to capture intermittent high-grade AV block
Correct hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia which may be exacerbating conduction abnormalities
Recognize that the brief convulsive movements represent convulsive syncope, not epileptic seizures
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs based on misdiagnosis of convulsive syncope as seizure
Discharging the patient without cardiac monitoring and pacemaker evaluation
Continuing hydrochlorothiazide without addressing the electrolyte depletion
Administering drugs that suppress AV conduction (beta-blockers, non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers, digoxin, adenosine)
Performing lumbar puncture (no indication for CSF analysis)
Attributing the episodes to vasovagal syncope without adequate cardiac workup
Allowing the patient to drive (commercial truck driver) before pacemaker implantation and clearance
key_reasoning_points: The brief convulsive movements lasting 10-15 seconds represent convulsive syncope (myoclonic jerks from transient cerebral hypoperfusion) rather than an epileptic seizure, which is a common diagnostic pitfall
The absence of prolonged post-ictal confusion (recovery within 30-60 seconds), tongue biting, and urinary incontinence argues against epilepsy
Bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) combined with first-degree AV block constitutes trifascicular disease, indicating widespread His-Purkinje system degeneration
The trifascicular disease pattern on ECG carries a significant risk of progression to intermittent or permanent complete heart block, which is the likely cause of the recurrent syncope
The paroxysmal nature of the high-grade AV block explains why the resting ECG does not show complete heart block and why prolonged monitoring is essential to capture the arrhythmia
Mild hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia from hydrochlorothiazide may be aggravating the conduction abnormalities and should be corrected
The patient's occupation as a long-haul truck driver adds urgency to the diagnosis, as recurrent syncope while driving poses a serious public safety risk
The elevated BNP and mildly reduced LVEF suggest chronic subclinical cardiac dysfunction, supporting the cardiac origin of symptoms

clinical_pearls: Convulsive syncope (brief myoclonic jerks from cerebral hypoperfusion during syncope) is frequently misdiagnosed as epileptic seizure and is the most common cause of seizure misdiagnosis
Trifascicular block (bifascicular block + first-degree AV block) on ECG is a high-risk pattern that should prompt inpatient monitoring and cardiology consultation for pacemaker evaluation
Mobitz type II AV block is an infra-nodal block that characteristically does not improve with atropine or exercise, distinguishing it from Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) which is AV nodal
Exercise-induced worsening of AV block is pathognomonic for infra-Hisian disease, as AV nodal block improves with exercise due to increased sympathetic tone
Thiazide diuretics can cause hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, which may exacerbate cardiac conduction abnormalities
